title: Laurelin
text:
Preface
Calculus made sense to me in a way that most mathematics did not. Not because it was easy — it was not — but because it described things that changed. Velocity and acceleration. The area under a curve. The box dimensions that maximize volume while minimizing material. I remember thinking: if I can write an equation for a falling object, I should be able to write one for sales figures, service orders, or the demand forecasts that actually drive business decisions.
I could not. I did not know where to start.
The equations we worked with in calculus had tidy, well-behaved variables. The variables I cared about — orders, failures, costs — did not come pre-packaged as functions. I did not know how to turn a spreadsheet into mathematics. I took a course that covered regression and did not recognize it as the tool I had been looking for. I finished my undergraduate degree in financial engineering with the vague sense that the mathematics I had learned was somehow adjacent to the problems I wanted to solve, but not quite touching them.
That gap stayed with me for a long time.
I went to graduate school in economics knowing almost nothing about the field. What drew me in was the mathematics — more of it, and different from what I had seen in financial engineering. I had always found it easy to develop an interest in new areas when the underlying structure was rigorous, and economics turned out to have plenty of that. I started working seriously with data — analysis, visualization, pattern recognition. The original question, the one about describing real business variables with equations, receded into the background. It felt like a different project.
It was not until my PhD that I realized I had been circling the same problem the entire time. Optimization, it turns out, is precisely the discipline of writing equations that describe what we want and finding the inputs that satisfy them. Machine learning is, at its core, the application of that idea to data. The regression I had not recognized as useful in my undergraduate years was the same regression I was now extending, formalizing, and building new variants of for my dissertation.
This book is my attempt to build that connection explicitly — to draw the straight line between calculus and ML that took me twenty years to see.

The mathematics here start from first principles and build toward the contributions of my PhD thesis: a family of Support Vector Regression models that optimize percentage-error loss functions directly. The early chapters require nothing beyond multivariable calculus. The later chapters are graduate-level, but the goal throughout is the same: to show where the equations come from and why they take the form they do.
Code examples appear in paired R and Python tabs. Advanced derivations are in collapsible sections — they are there for completeness, but skipping them does not break the argument.

title: 1  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong
text:
1.1 A decision you make every day
There are two kinds of forecasters: those who can’t predict the future, and those who don’t know they can’t predict the future.
The first group has an advantage. They stop trying to be right and start trying to be less wrong — and that turns out to be a problem you can actually solve.
You are running out of coffee at home. You need to decide how many bags to buy. Buy too few and you run out before the week ends. Buy too many and they go stale before you finish them. Neither is catastrophic, but both have a cost.
Without thinking about it, you are solving an optimization problem. You have a loss — the combined cost of under-buying and over-buying — and you are trying to minimize it given what you know: your consumption rate, how long bags last, how often you go to the store.
Now scale that up. You are not buying coffee for one person. You are managing the electricity grid for an entire region. Every thirty minutes, you need to decide how much power to procure for the next interval. Buy too little and you impose rolling blackouts. Buy too much and you pay for energy that goes nowhere. The cost of being wrong is not stale coffee — it is millions of dollars and compromised infrastructure.
The structure of the problem is the same. The stakes are not.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization
1  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong

title: 1  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong
text:
1.2 Two datasets, one question
This book uses two datasets as its main thread. They come from different domains and have different structures, but they share the same underlying question: given what you can observe, what is your best prediction of what you cannot?

1.2.1 Victorian electricity demand
vic_elec records half-hourly electricity demand and temperature across the state of Victoria, Australia, from 2012 to 2014. Every row is a moment. Every moment is a decision someone had to make about how much power to have available.


Code
library(tsibbledata)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(here)

vic_elec <- readr::read_csv(here("data", "vic_elec.csv"),
                             show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(Time = as.POSIXct(Time))

vic_sample <- vic_elec |>
  filter(Time >= as.POSIXct("2014-01-01"),
         Time <  as.POSIXct("2014-01-15"))

p_demand <- ggplot(vic_sample, aes(x = Time, y = Demand)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2166ac", linewidth = 0.5) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Demand (MW)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

p_temp <- ggplot(vic_sample, aes(x = Time, y = Temperature)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#d6604d", linewidth = 0.5) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

p_demand / p_temp








Figure 1.1: Half-hourly electricity demand (top) and temperature (bottom) in Victoria, Australia, January 2014. The relationship is clear but imprecise — temperature alone does not determine demand.





Temperature drives demand — air conditioning is the dominant load in an Australian summer. But the relationship shifts. A hot Tuesday looks different from a hot Saturday. A heat wave on the third consecutive day draws more than a heat wave on the first. The signal is real; it is just not simple enough to write down by hand.

RPython




Code
vic_elec |>
  select(Time, Demand, Temperature, Holiday) |>
  slice_head(n = 8) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2,
               caption = "First eight rows of `vic_elec`.")

from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd

def _project_root(markers=("_quarto.yml", "renv.lock")):

vic_elec[["Time", "Demand", "Temperature", "Holiday"]].head(8)

1.2.2 California housing prices
The second dataset is 20,640 California census blocks from the early 1990s. Each block is described by eight numbers: median income, house age, average rooms, average bedrooms, population, average occupancy, latitude, longitude. The target is median house value.


Code
library(ggplot2)
library(here)

California <- readr::read_csv(here("data", "california_housing.csv"),
                               show_col_types = FALSE)

ggplot(California, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude,
                       colour = log(median_house_value))) +
  geom_point(size = 0.3, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "log(value)") +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1.3) +
  labs(x = "Longitude", y = "Latitude") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)








Figure 1.2: Median house value across California census blocks. Coastal proximity matters, but income, density, and other variables all play a role. No single feature explains the target.





There is no time axis here. Census blocks do not arrive in sequence — each one is independent. What matters is not what happened before, but which other blocks are similar and whether similar blocks tend to have similar prices. The coastal premium is visible in the plot. So is the concentration of high-value blocks in the Bay Area and Los Angeles. But the relationship between the eight features and the target is not something you can read off a map.

crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization
1  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong

title: 1  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong
section: 1.3 Why a formula is not enough
text:
1.3 Why a formula is not enough
When you write =0.3 * B2 + 85000 in a spreadsheet, you have already made the decision. You chose the slope. You chose the intercept. The formula does what you told it to do, nothing more.
For the electricity problem, you do not know the slope in advance. You do not even know that the relationship is linear. The only honest starting point is the data itself — and the question of what it means for a prediction to be good.
That question has a precise answer. You choose a way to measure how wrong a prediction is. Then you find the function that is least wrong, on average, over all the data you have. That search is optimization. Every model in this book is the result of that search, with different choices about what “wrong” means and what functions are allowed.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization
1  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong

title: 1  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong
text:
1.4 What comes next
Part I builds the optimization machinery from scratch. You will see how to find the minimum of a function when there are no constraints, then when there are. You will learn to read the conditions that tell you whether a solution is optimal. By the end, you will have the tools to derive models from first principles — not just use them.
Parts II and III introduce the models themselves: how to measure similarity between observations, and how to use that similarity to build predictions that generalize beyond the training data.
The final chapters return to these two datasets with models that optimize percentage error directly — a choice that matters when a $50,000 prediction error means something very different for a $100,000 house than for a $600,000 house.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization
1  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong

title: 1  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong
text:
1.5 Exercises






NoteExercise 1.1 — Reading the time series





Load vic_elec and compute the average half-hourly demand separately for weekdays and weekends. Plot both on the same axis. At what hour of the day is the gap between weekday and weekend demand largest?









NoteExercise 1.2 — The cost of being wrong





Suppose electricity costs $150 per MWh during peak hours (5–9 PM on weekdays) and $40 per MWh at all other times. Using vic_elec:

Compute the total procurement cost if you always buy exactly what was demanded.
Compute the cost if your forecast is 10% high during peak hours (you over-procure; the excess is wasted).
Compute the cost if your forecast is 10% low during peak hours (you under-procure; you must buy the deficit at a spot premium of $500 per MWh).

Which direction of error is more expensive? Does your answer change how you would define a good prediction?









NoteExercise 1.3 — What does “wrong” mean?





For the California Housing data, compute prediction errors for two naive models: one that always predicts the training mean, and one that always predicts $200,000.
Compute MAE, RMSE, and MAPE for each. Which metric best captures whether the model is useful? Does a $50,000 error mean the same thing for a $100,000 house and a $600,000 house?









NoteExercise 1.4





A loss function \(\ell(\hat{y}, y)\) is scale-free if multiplying both the prediction and the target by the same positive constant leaves the loss unchanged: \(\ell(c\hat{y}, cy) = \ell(\hat{y}, y)\) for all \(c > 0\).
Show that squared error \((\hat{y} - y)^2\) is not scale-free. Then construct a loss function that is. Is it unique? What structural property do all scale-free loss functions share?
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization
1  The Art of Being Systematically Less Wrong

title: 2  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
2.1 Derivatives and the direction of change
Every warehouse manager knows the feeling. Order too little and you run out of stock mid-week, lose sales, and scramble to reorder at a premium. Order too much and you pay to store inventory nobody needs, watch it depreciate, and tie up capital that could go elsewhere. Somewhere between those two failures is the right number — and finding it does not require guessing.
The total cost of managing inventory has two competing pieces. Holding cost grows with order quantity: the more units you store, the more you spend on warehouse space, insurance, and opportunity cost. Ordering cost goes the other way: the more you order per shipment, the fewer shipments you make and the less you spend on delivery and setup fees.
Let \(q\) denote the order quantity, \(D\) the annual demand, \(h\) the holding cost per unit per year, and \(s\) the fixed cost per order. The classical Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) model combines these into a total annual cost:
\[
C(q) = \frac{hq}{2} + \frac{Ds}{q},
\qquad q > 0.
\tag{2.1}\]
The first term increases linearly with \(q\); the second decreases hyperbolically. Their sum produces a U-shaped curve with a single minimum — the kind of structure where calculus is exactly the right tool.
The derivative \(C'(q)\) measures how \(C\) changes when \(q\) changes by an infinitesimal amount. At any point \(q_0\):
\[
C'(q_0) = \lim_{\varepsilon \to 0} \frac{C(q_0 + \varepsilon) -
C(q_0)}{\varepsilon}.
\]
If \(C'(q_0) > 0\), the cost increases as \(q\) increases — we are to the right of the minimum. If \(C'(q_0) < 0\), it decreases — we are to the left. The derivative encodes both direction and rate of change.
For the EOQ cost:
\[
C'(q) = \frac{h}{2} - \frac{Ds}{q^2}.
\]
In several variables, the derivative generalizes to the gradient:
\[
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \left[\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1},\
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2},\ \ldots,\ \frac{\partial f}{\partial
x_p}\right]^\top.
\]
The gradient at \(\mathbf{x}_0\) points in the direction of steepest ascent of \(f\) from that point. Its magnitude measures how rapidly \(f\) changes in that direction. This geometric picture is the foundation of every first-order optimization method we will encounter in this book.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 2  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
TipThe derivative as slope and direction



Think of the derivative at a point as the slope of the tangent line to the curve at that point. A positive slope means “going uphill to the right.” A negative slope means “going downhill to the right.” Zero slope means the tangent is flat — we are at a critical point.
In multiple dimensions, the gradient \(\nabla f\) generalizes the derivative: it is a vector pointing in the direction of steepest ascent. Moving opposite to the gradient — downhill — brings us closer to a minimum.






RPython




Code
# EOQ parameters
1D <- 10000   # annual demand (units)
2h <- 2.5     # holding cost ($/unit/year)
3s <- 200     # ordering cost ($/order)

q_vals <- seq(100, 4000, by = 10)

eoq_df <- tibble(
  q          = q_vals,
4  holding    = h * q / 2,
5  ordering   = D * s / q,
6  total      = holding + ordering
) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = c(holding, ordering, total),
    names_to  = "component",
    values_to = "cost"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    component = factor(
      component,
      levels = c("holding", "ordering", "total"),
      labels = c("Holding cost", "Ordering cost", "Total cost")
    )
  )

# Optimal q*
7q_star <- sqrt(2 * D * s / h)

p_base <- ggplot(eoq_df, aes(x = q, y = cost, color = component, linetype = component)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_linetype_manual(
    values = c("Holding cost" = "solid", "Ordering cost" = "solid",
               "Total cost"   = "solid")
  ) +
  labs(
    x     = "Order quantity (units)",
    y     = "Annual cost ($)",
    color = NULL, linetype = NULL
  )

# Reference line + annotation added per render so colours adapt to mode
add_refs <- function(pl, dark = FALSE) {
  pl +
    geom_vline(xintercept = q_star, linetype = "dashed",
               color = lc("ref_line", dark = dark)) +
    annotate(
      "text", x = q_star + 80, y = max(eoq_df$cost) * 0.85,
      label = paste0("q* = ", round(q_star)), hjust = 0, size = 3.5,
      color = lc("ref_text", dark = dark)
    )
}



1

Annual demand — how many units the warehouse moves in a year.

2

Cost to hold one unit in inventory for one year.

3

Fixed cost per replenishment order (delivery, setup, paperwork).

4

Holding cost grows linearly with order quantity.

5

Ordering cost decreases hyperbolically: more per order means fewer orders.

6

Total cost: the U-shaped sum of two competing terms.

7

The analytical optimum — we will derive this in the next section.

























Figure 2.1: EOQ total cost as a function of order quantity. The holding and ordering cost components are shown separately. The minimum occurs where the two components intersect.







Code
# EOQ parameters
1D = 10_000   # annual demand (units)
2h = 2.5      # holding cost ($/unit/year)
3s = 200      # ordering cost ($/order)

q_vals = np.linspace(100, 4000, 500)
4holding  = h * q_vals / 2
5ordering = D * s / q_vals
6total    = holding + ordering

7q_star = np.sqrt(2 * D * s / h)

plt.style.use("seaborn-v0_8-whitegrid")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor("#ffffff")

ax.plot(q_vals, holding,  label="Holding cost",  color=lc("green"),  lw=1.5)
ax.plot(q_vals, ordering, label="Ordering cost", color=lc("blue"),   lw=1.5)
ax.plot(q_vals, total,    label="Total cost",    color=lc("orange"), lw=1.5)
ax.axvline(q_star, color=lc("ref_line"), linestyle="--", lw=1)
ax.text(q_star + 50, total.max() * 0.85,
        f"q* = {q_star:.0f}", fontsize=10, color=lc("ref_text"))

ax.set_xlabel("Order quantity (units)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("Annual cost ($)",        fontsize=12)
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(ticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x:,.0f}"))
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.close()



1

Annual demand — same parameters as the R version.

2

Holding cost per unit per year.

3

Fixed cost per order.

4

Holding cost: linear in \(q\).

5

Ordering cost: inversely proportional to \(q\).

6

Total cost: sum of the two competing components.

7

The analytical optimum \(q^* = \sqrt{2Ds/h}\).









EOQ total cost as a function of order quantity. The holding and ordering cost components are shown separately. The minimum occurs where the two components intersect.







EOQ total cost as a function of order quantity. The holding and ordering cost components are shown separately. The minimum occurs where the two components intersect.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 2  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
2.2 First-order optimality conditions
The U-shape of \(C(q)\) in Figure 2.1 reveals the key idea: at the minimum, the curve has zero slope. The holding cost is rising at exactly the rate the ordering cost is falling. Neither term dominates — they balance.
This balance is not a coincidence. It is a consequence of the first-order optimality condition: at a local minimum \(q^*\) of a differentiable function, the derivative must vanish.






ImportantFirst-order necessary condition



Let \(f : \mathbb{R}^p \to \mathbb{R}\) be differentiable. If \(\mathbf{x}^*\) is a local minimum of \(f\), then
\[
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}.
\]
In one dimension: \(f'(x^*) = 0\).


This condition is necessary but not sufficient on its own — a zero gradient identifies a candidate, not a confirmed minimum. A maximum and a saddle point also satisfy \(\nabla f = 0\).
For the EOQ cost, setting \(C'(q) = 0\):
\[
\frac{h}{2} - \frac{Ds}{q^2} = 0
\implies
q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2Ds}{h}}.
\tag{2.2}\]
With \(D = 10{,}000\), \(s = 200\), and \(h = 2.5\), this gives \(q^* = \sqrt{1{,}600{,}000} = 1{,}265\) units — the vertical line in Figure 2.1.






WarningCommon confusion: necessary ≠ sufficient



The condition \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}\) is necessary for a local minimum — but a zero gradient alone does not confirm that you have found one. The function \(f(x) = x^3\) satisfies \(f'(0) = 0\), yet \(x = 0\) is an inflection point, not a minimum. Always verify second-order conditions or use structural properties (such as convexity) to confirm.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 2  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
2.3 Critical points and second-order conditions
A point where \(\nabla f = \mathbf{0}\) is called a critical point. Three geometric possibilities exist: a minimum, a maximum, or a saddle point. The second derivative determines which.
In one dimension, the second derivative \(f''(x^*)\) measures how quickly the slope is changing at the critical point:

\(f''(x^*) > 0\): the slope is increasing — the curve is concave up. The critical point is a local minimum.
\(f''(x^*) < 0\): the slope is decreasing — the curve is concave down. The critical point is a local maximum.
\(f''(x^*) = 0\): the test is inconclusive. Higher derivatives must be examined.

For the EOQ cost, \(C''(q) = 2Ds/q^3 > 0\) for all \(q > 0\). The critical point at \(q^*\) is confirmed as a minimum.
In multiple variables, the second-order information is encoded in the Hessian matrix \(H_f(\mathbf{x})\), whose \((i,j)\)-th entry is the mixed partial derivative:
\[
[H_f(\mathbf{x})]_{i,j} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i\, \partial x_j}.
\]
The Hessian plays the role of the second derivative: it captures the curvature of \(f\) in every direction simultaneously.






ImportantSecond-order optimality conditions



Let \(\mathbf{x}^*\) satisfy \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}\).

Sufficient for local minimum: \(H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)\) is positive definite — that is, \(\mathbf{v}^\top H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)
\mathbf{v} > 0\) for all \(\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}\).
Sufficient for local maximum: \(H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)\) is negative definite.
Saddle point: \(H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)\) is indefinite — positive in some directions, negative in others.



Positive definiteness of the Hessian is the multivariable generalization of \(f'' > 0\). Checking it amounts to verifying that all eigenvalues of \(H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)\) are positive — or equivalently, that all leading principal minors of \(H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)\) are positive (Sylvester’s criterion).






NoteDerivation: classifying critical points via the Hessian





A quadratic Taylor expansion of \(f\) around a critical point \(\mathbf{x}^*\) gives:
\[
f(\mathbf{x}^* + \mathbf{v}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \underbrace{
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^\top \mathbf{v}}_{=\,0} +
\frac{1}{2}\, \mathbf{v}^\top H_f(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{v}.
\]
The first-order term vanishes at a critical point. The sign of the remainder depends entirely on \(\mathbf{v}^\top H_f(\mathbf{x}^*)
\mathbf{v}\):

Positive for every nonzero \(\mathbf{v}\) (positive definite Hessian) \(\Rightarrow\) \(f(\mathbf{x}^* + \mathbf{v}) > f(\mathbf{x}^*)\) in every direction \(\Rightarrow\) local minimum.
Negative for every nonzero \(\mathbf{v}\) (negative definite Hessian) \(\Rightarrow\) local maximum.
Mixed sign (indefinite Hessian) \(\Rightarrow\) the function increases in some directions and decreases in others from \(\mathbf{x}^*\) \(\Rightarrow\) saddle point.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 2  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
2.4 Convexity and global minima
The second-order conditions guarantee a local minimum — a point better than its immediate neighbors. For optimization in practice, we usually want a global minimum. And local methods, including gradient descent, can get stuck in local minima if the function has the wrong shape.
Convexity is what prevents this.






ImportantConvexity



A function \(f : \mathbb{R}^p \to \mathbb{R}\) is convex if for any two points \(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}\) and any \(\lambda \in [0, 1]\):
\[
f\!\left(\lambda \mathbf{x} + (1-\lambda)\mathbf{y}\right)
\leq \lambda f(\mathbf{x}) + (1-\lambda) f(\mathbf{y}).
\tag{2.3}\]
Geometrically: the function lies below or on the chord connecting any two points on its graph.


This definition has a powerful consequence: a convex function has no local minima that are not also global. If you find a point where \(\nabla f = \mathbf{0}\) and \(f\) is convex, you have found the global minimum — there is nothing better anywhere.






TipGeometric picture: the bowl



A strictly convex function looks like a bowl. Any ball you place inside rolls to the bottom — the unique global minimum. A non-convex function has bumps and valleys; the ball may settle in a local depression without ever reaching the lowest point.
For convex functions, the first-order condition \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)
= \mathbf{0}\) is not just necessary — it is also sufficient for a global minimum.


The EOQ cost \(C(q) = hq/2 + Ds/q\) is convex on \(q > 0\): its second derivative \(C''(q) = 2Ds/q^3\) is strictly positive everywhere on the domain. The critical point at \(q^*\) is therefore the unique global minimum. No other order quantity can do better.
Several equivalent conditions characterize convexity:

Via second derivative (one dimension): \(f\) is convex if and only if \(f''(x) \geq 0\) for all \(x\) in the domain.
Via Hessian (multiple dimensions): \(f\) is convex if and only if \(H_f(\mathbf{x})\) is positive semidefinite for all \(\mathbf{x}\) in the domain.
Strict convexity: replace \(\leq\) with \(<\) in Equation 2.3 (for \(\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{y}\)) — this guarantees uniqueness of the minimum.







TipWhy convexity matters for optimization



Most objectives we will encounter in this book are convex by construction. This is not accidental — the models are designed that way deliberately. Convexity guarantees that any point satisfying the first-order conditions is a global minimum, not merely a local one. There is no risk of a solver finding a bad solution that happens to satisfy \(\nabla f = \mathbf{0}\). When we arrive at the optimization problems in Part III, this property will be the reason we can trust the solutions we compute.


The sum of two convex functions is convex, and a nonnegative scalar multiple of a convex function is convex. These preservation rules allow us to certify convexity for complex objectives by decomposing them into simpler pieces.

RPython




Code
D <- 10000; h <- 2.5; s <- 200

q_vals <- seq(200, 3000, by = 5)
cost   <- tibble(
  q    = q_vals,
  cost = h * q / 2 + D * s / q
)

# Two demonstration points
q1 <- 500;  q2 <- 2200
c1 <- h * q1 / 2 + D * s / q1
c2 <- h * q2 / 2 + D * s / q2

# Chord between the two points
chord <- tibble(q = c(q1, q2), cost = c(c1, c2))

p_base <- ggplot(cost, aes(x = q, y = cost)) +
  geom_line(color = lc("green"), linewidth = 1) +
1  geom_line(data = chord, aes(x = q, y = cost),
            color = lc("red"), linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(data = chord, aes(x = q, y = cost),
             color = lc("red"), size = 3) +
  labs(
    x = "Order quantity (units)",
    y = "Annual cost ($)"
  )

# Point labels added per render so text colour adapts to mode
add_labels <- function(pl, dark = FALSE) {
  pl +
    annotate("text", x = q1 - 80, y = c1 + 150,
             label = "(q₁, C(q₁))", size = 3.5, hjust = 1,
             color = lc("ref_text", dark = dark)) +
    annotate("text", x = q2 + 80, y = c2 + 150,
             label = "(q₂, C(q₂))", size = 3.5, hjust = 0,
             color = lc("ref_text", dark = dark))
}



1

The chord lies above the curve between \(q_1\) and \(q_2\) — this is the geometric definition of convexity.

























Figure 2.2: Geometric interpretation of convexity. For any two points on the EOQ cost curve, the chord (dashed) lies entirely above the curve — the defining property of a convex function.







Code
D = 10_000; h = 2.5; s = 200

q_vals = np.linspace(200, 3000, 500)
cost   = h * q_vals / 2 + D * s / q_vals

# Two demonstration points
q1, q2 = 500, 2200
c1 = h * q1 / 2 + D * s / q1
c2 = h * q2 / 2 + D * s / q2

plt.style.use("seaborn-v0_8-whitegrid")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor("#ffffff")

ax.plot(q_vals, cost, color=lc("green"), lw=1.5, label="Total cost")
1ax.plot([q1, q2], [c1, c2], color=lc("red"), lw=1.5,
        linestyle="--", label="Chord")
ax.scatter([q1, q2], [c1, c2], color=lc("red"), s=50, zorder=5)

ax.annotate(r"$(q_1, C(q_1))$", xy=(q1, c1),
            xytext=(q1 - 100, c1 + 200), fontsize=10,
            ha="right", arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color=lc("ref_line")))
ax.annotate(r"$(q_2, C(q_2))$", xy=(q2, c2),
            xytext=(q2 + 100, c2 + 200), fontsize=10,
            ha="left",  arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color=lc("ref_line")))

ax.set_xlabel("Order quantity (units)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("Annual cost ($)",        fontsize=12)
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.close()



1

The chord lies entirely above the EOQ curve between \(q_1\) and \(q_2\), confirming convexity.









Geometric interpretation of convexity. For any two points on the EOQ cost curve, the chord (dashed) lies entirely above the curve — the defining property of a convex function.







Geometric interpretation of convexity. For any two points on the EOQ cost curve, the chord (dashed) lies entirely above the curve — the defining property of a convex function.
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 2  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
2.5 Gradient descent: geometric intuition
When a closed-form solution to \(\nabla f = \mathbf{0}\) exists — as it does for the EOQ — calculus gives us the answer directly. The derivative equation is solved in closed form, and we are done. But most objective functions encountered in machine learning have no closed-form minimizer. The equation \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}\) may be nonlinear or involve thousands of unknowns that must be solved numerically.
Gradient descent is the simplest numerical strategy. Its logic is purely geometric: the gradient \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x})\) points uphill. Moving in the direction \(-\nabla f(\mathbf{x})\) — the negative gradient — takes us downhill.
Starting from an initial point \(\mathbf{x}_0\), one gradient descent step produces:
\[
\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 - \eta\, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0),
\tag{2.4}\]
where \(\eta > 0\) is the step size (also called the learning rate). The step size controls how far we move along the downhill direction.
On the EOQ cost, one step from \(q_0 = 2{,}000\) with \(\eta = 0.05\):
\[
C'(2000) = \frac{2.5}{2} - \frac{10000 \times 200}{2000^2} = 1.25 - 0.5 = 0.75.
\]
\[
q_1 = 2000 - 0.05 \times 0.75 = 1999.96.
\]
That is a small step — the derivative has units of dollars per unit, and \(\eta\) must be calibrated accordingly. The point is not the arithmetic; it is the direction. The gradient told us we were too far right (positive derivative means we should decrease \(q\)), and the step corrects course.






TipWhat gradient descent does and does not do



Gradient descent, as described here, is a geometric idea — not a complete algorithm. The one-step picture in Equation 2.4 captures the logic. Repeated application moves steadily toward the minimum.
What we have not specified: how to choose \(\eta\), when to stop, how to handle noisy or high-dimensional settings. Those details matter enormously in practice and are the subject of Chapter 6, which covers first- and second-order numerical methods in full. The purpose here is only geometric: the gradient tells you where uphill is, and you go the other way.



RPython




Code
D <- 10000; h <- 2.5; s <- 200
1C  <- function(q) h * q / 2 + D * s / q
2Cp <- function(q) h / 2 - D * s / q^2

q0   <- 2000
3eta  <- 100   # step size in units of q (illustrative)
grad <- Cp(q0)
q1   <- q0 - eta * grad

q_vals <- seq(400, 3200, by = 5)
cost_df <- tibble(q = q_vals, cost = C(q_vals))

arrow_df <- tibble(
  q    = c(q0, q1),
  cost = C(c(q0, q1))
)

p_base <- ggplot(cost_df, aes(x = q, y = cost)) +
  geom_line(color = lc("green"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = q0, y = C(q0), xend = q1, yend = C(q0)),
    arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed"),
    color = lc("red"), linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_point(aes(x = q0, y = C(q0)),
4             color = lc("blue"), size = 3) +
  geom_point(aes(x = q1, y = C(q1)),
5             color = lc("red"), size = 3) +
  labs(x = "Order quantity (units)", y = "Annual cost ($)")

# Step labels added per render so text colour adapts to mode
add_labels <- function(pl, dark = FALSE) {
  pl +
    annotate("text", x = q0 + 50, y = C(q0) + 60,
             label = expression(q[0] == 2000), hjust = 0, size = 3.5,
             color = lc("ref_text", dark = dark)) +
    annotate("text", x = q1 - 50, y = C(q1) + 60,
             label = expression(q[1]), hjust = 1, size = 3.5,
             color = lc("ref_text", dark = dark))
}



1

The EOQ cost function \(C(q) = hq/2 + Ds/q\).

2

Its derivative \(C'(q) = h/2 - Ds/q^2\).

3

Step size chosen to produce a visible move in the plot — not a physically optimal learning rate.

4

Starting point \(q_0\).

5

One step later: \(q_1 = q_0 - \eta C'(q_0)\).

























Figure 2.3: One step of gradient descent on the EOQ cost. Starting at \(q_0 = 2000\), the negative gradient points left — toward the minimum. The step lands at \(q_1\).







Code
D = 10_000; h = 2.5; s = 200
1C  = lambda q: h * q / 2 + D * s / q
2Cp = lambda q: h / 2 - D * s / q**2

q0  = 2000
3eta = 100
q1  = q0 - eta * Cp(q0)

q_vals = np.linspace(400, 3200, 500)

plt.style.use("seaborn-v0_8-whitegrid")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor("#ffffff")

ax.plot(q_vals, C(q_vals), color=lc("green"), lw=1.5)

ax.annotate(
    "", xy=(q1, C(q0)), xytext=(q0, C(q0)),
    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=lc("red"), lw=1.5)
4)
ax.scatter([q0], [C(q0)], color=lc("blue"), s=60, zorder=5,
           label=r"$q_0 = 2000$")
ax.scatter([q1], [C(q1)], color=lc("red"), s=60, zorder=5,
           label=r"$q_1$")

ax.set_xlabel("Order quantity (units)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("Annual cost ($)",        fontsize=12)
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.close()



1

EOQ cost function as a Python lambda.

2

Its derivative.

3

Illustrative step size — calibrated for visual clarity, not numerical optimality.

4

Arrow shows the gradient descent move: left, toward the minimum.









One step of gradient descent on the EOQ cost. Starting at \(q_0 = 2000\), the negative gradient points left — toward the minimum. The step lands at \(q_1\).







One step of gradient descent on the EOQ cost. Starting at \(q_0 = 2000\), the negative gradient points left — toward the minimum. The step lands at \(q_1\).
crumbs: Part I: The Language of Optimization

title: 2  Unconstrained Optimization
text:
2.6 Exercises
Exercise 2.1. A server provisioning problem has the following response time cost:
\[
L(n) = \frac{a}{n} + b \cdot n^2, \qquad n > 0,
\]
where \(n\) is the number of allocated servers, \(a > 0\) captures the throughput benefit of more servers, and \(b > 0\) captures the coordination overhead that grows super-linearly.

Find the first-order optimality condition and solve for \(n^*\).
Verify that \(n^*\) is a minimum using the second-order condition.
Is \(L(n)\) convex on \(n > 0\)? Justify using the second derivative.


Exercise 2.2. Let \(f(x, y) = (x - 2)^2 + (y - 3)^2 + xy\).

Find all critical points by solving \(\nabla f = \mathbf{0}\).
Compute the Hessian \(H_f\) and classify each critical point.
Is \(f\) convex? Provide a criterion-based justification.


Exercise 2.3. The gradient descent update \(x_{t+1} = x_t - \eta f'(x_t)\) applied to \(f(x) = x^2\) starting from \(x_0 = 10\):

Show that after \(t\) steps, \(x_t = x_0 (1 - 2\eta)^t\).
For what range of \(\eta\) does the sequence converge to the minimum?
What happens when \(\eta = 1\)? When \(\eta > 1\)?


Exercise 2.4 — Graduate level. Consider a function \(f : \mathbb{R}^p \to \mathbb{R}\) that is differentiable and convex.

Prove that any local minimum of \(f\) is also a global minimum. (Hint: suppose \(\mathbf{x}^*\) is a local minimum and \(\mathbf{y}\) satisfies \(f(\mathbf{y}) < f(\mathbf{x}^*)\). Derive a contradiction using the convexity definition.)
Show that if \(f\) is strictly convex, the global minimum is unique.
Now suppose \(f\) is convex and \(\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}\). Prove, using the first-order characterization of convexity (\(f(\mathbf{y}) \geq f(\mathbf{x}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^\top
(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x})\)), that \(\mathbf{x}^*\) is a global minimum.
